definition: The quality of being clever; quick-witted intelligence or skill.
example: Cleverness is a gift, kindness is a choice. Gifts are easy — they're given after all.
source: Princeton Baccalaureate Address - 'We Are What We Choose'
speech: We Are What We Choose (Princeton Baccalaureate)

definition: The quality of being friendly, generous, and considerate.
example: Cleverness is a gift, kindness is a choice.
source: Princeton Baccalaureate Address - 'We Are What We Choose'
speech: We Are What We Choose (Princeton Baccalaureate)

definition: A feeling of sadness, repentance, or disappointment over something that has happened or been done.
example: Minimize regret: in the end we are our choices, so build yourself a story you will not wish to rewrite.
source: Princeton Baccalaureate Address - 'We Are What We Choose'
speech: We Are What We Choose (Princeton Baccalaureate)

definition: The practice of making decisions with a focus on outcomes that will occur far in the future.
example: Long-termism asks what we will do with our gifts—and that answer will define us more than any quarter’s results.
source: Princeton Baccalaureate Address - 'We Are What We Choose'
speech: We Are What We Choose (Princeton Baccalaureate)

definition: The action of inventing something, typically a process or device.
example: Invention is hard because gifts are easy; choices demand courage, and great companies choose to invent.
source: Princeton Baccalaureate Address - 'We Are What We Choose'
speech: We Are What We Choose (Princeton Baccalaureate)

definition: The quality of being frugal; careful management of resources, especially money or materials.
example: Frugality is a core principle. We spend money on things that matter to customers.

definition: The act, state, or right of possessing something; leaders act on behalf of the entire company.
example: Ownership means thinking long term and refusing to sacrifice long-term value for short-term results.

definition: A system or standard of measurement; data used to track performance.
example: We start with the customer and work backwards, using metrics that matter to the customer experience.

definition: The state of being obsessed with someone or something; an idea or thought that continually preoccupies or intrudes on a person's mind.
example: Customer obsession treats guests as invited to a party where we are the hosts—our job is their delight every day.

definition: A period or state of inactivity or equilibrium; a slowing or stoppage of growth or development.
example: Day 2 is stasis. Followed by irrelevance. Followed by excruciating, painful decline. Followed by death.

definition: A person who buys goods or services; the focus of a business.
example: Start with the customer and work backwards—every process is a servant of that end.

definition: A mindset of permanent beginning: urgency, invention, and refusal of stagnation.
example: Day-one culture resists day-two stagnation—bureaucracy, proxies, and resting on momentum.

definition: A stand-in measure used instead of the real goal (often dangerously).
example: When a metric becomes a proxy for the mission, people optimize the number and forget the customer.

definition: To create or design something that has not existed before.
example: If you invent only what is safe, you will never hear the future arrive.

definition: The capacity to accept delay without anger; long-horizon endurance.
example: Patience is a strategy when the flywheel needs years, not quarters, to turn.

definition: Demanding criteria for quality that raise the floor for everyone.
example: High-standards are contagious: people either rise to them or leave the room.
